items.mistake: Reaching for greedy without proving (or sanity-checking) the choice property.
items.fix: Try a small counter-example. If you can construct one where greedy is sub-optimal, switch to DP or backtracking.
items.why: Greedy fails silently — it returns a valid-looking answer that isn't best, and unit tests can pass by luck.
items.example:
```python
# Counter-example for greedy coin change:
greedy_coin_change([1, 3, 4], 6)   # returns 3; optimal is 2
```

items.mistake: Picking the wrong greedy criterion (e.g. sorting by start time instead of end time for activity selection).
items.fix: State the local choice and verify it leaves room for the rest.
items.why: Two greedies on the same problem can have wildly different worst cases.
items.example:
```python
# Sort by END time (correct):
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
# Sort by START time (incorrect; can miss many compatible activities):
```

items.mistake: Using greedy decoding without comparing to other strategies.
items.fix: Evaluate against sampling, top-k / top-p, beam search; pick by your actual quality metric and budget.
items.why: Greedy decoding tends to repeat, drift to high-frequency tokens and produce dull outputs.
items.example:
```python
# Try multiple strategies and pick by eval:
for strategy in (greedy, top_p_0_9, beam_4):
    score = evaluate(strategy, eval_set)
```

items.fix: Brute force enumerates ALL options; greedy commits to ONE at each step. Different time complexities, different correctness guarantees.
items.why: Greedy is fast (linear/log-linear); brute force is exponential — they make different tradeoffs.
items.example:
```python
# Greedy: pick highest score each step, O(n log n).
# Brute force: try every subset, O(2ⁿ).
```
